title: Practice Exam 2
description: Practice exam emphasizing NPD opportunity analysis, conjoint analysis, and brand sustainability. Covers market sizing, RWW framework, part-worth utilities, relative importance, and brand repositioning strategies.

questions.question: In the Opportunity Tournament and Filtering Model, a firm that wants to improve the expected quality of its best surviving opportunity should focus on which four levers?

options.text: Price, promotion, product, and place (the 4 Ps of marketing)
Mean quality, variance in quality, number of opportunities, and evaluation accuracy
R&D budget, team size, time horizon, and executive sponsorship
Customer segmentation, technology readiness, competitive intensity, and regulatory fit

questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly identifies four factors that determine the quality of the best opportunity from a tournament: (1) mean quality of opportunities, (2) variance in quality, (3) number of opportunities generated, and (4) accuracy of evaluation. Improving any one of these improves expected output. The other options conflate unrelated frameworks.

questions.question: In Lecture 7, which of the following is NOT listed as a subdimension of Brand Imagery in the CBBE pyramid?

options.text: User profiles
Purchase and usage situations
Price and service effectiveness
Personality and values, and history/heritage/experiences

questions.explanation: Brand Imagery covers the extrinsic, intangible properties of the brand: user profiles; purchase and usage situations; personality and values; and history/heritage/experiences. Price and service effectiveness belong to Brand Performance (the functional, left-side Level-2 dimension), not Brand Imagery. A key distinction in the CBBE model is that Performance is rational/functional while Imagery is symbolic/emotional.

questions.question: P&G's Swiffer was developed after ethnographic researchers visited customers' homes and noticed their floors were already clean upon arrival. What latent need did this observation reveal?

options.text: Customers wanted a mop that was easier to rinse and wring out
Customers wanted floors that dried faster after mopping
Cleaning was emotionally tied to identity and social shame — particularly the difficulty of cleaning the broom itself
Customers preferred natural, chemical-free cleaning products over conventional detergents

questions.explanation: The key latent insight from the Swiffer ethnography was that floor cleanliness was a 'reflection of themselves' (identity and social shame), and the sub-observation that brooms were themselves hard to clean led to the disposable electrostatic pad concept. The real need was eliminating the maintenance burden of the cleaning tool, not improving the cleaning liquid or dry time.

questions.question: In the Nike yoga apparel market sizing example, the SAM (Serviceable Addressable Market) in Singapore was estimated at SGD 180 million. How was this figure derived?

options.text: 1,000,000 yoga consumers × SGD 180 average spend
SGD 300M TAM × 60% who are premium-branded buyers
SGD 300M TAM × 10% Nike capture rate
1,000,000 yoga consumers × 18% penetration rate × SGD 1,000 average spend

questions.explanation: SAM = TAM × the proportion of buyers who purchase premium branded yoga apparel. TAM was SGD 300M (1M consumers × SGD 300 avg spend), and 60% were estimated to buy premium branded products, giving SAM = SGD 300M × 60% = SGD 180M. The SGD 18M figure is the SOM (10% of SAM), not the SAM itself.

questions.question: In the Jobs To Be Done (JTBD) framework introduced in Lecture 3, how is the core framing question structured?

options.text: 'What features do customers want in this product category?'
'When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [desired outcome]'
'How can this product be made cheaper and faster than the competition?'
'Which customer segment has the highest willingness to pay for this solution?'

questions.explanation: JTBD reframes the question from 'what features do users want?' to what 'job' the customer is trying to accomplish. The structured format is: 'When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [desired outcome].' This anchors product development to the real situational trigger rather than surface-level feature requests or demographic profiling. The other options describe feature-centric, cost-reduction, or segmentation approaches that JTBD deliberately moves away from.

questions.question: PESTEL analysis and Porter's Five Forces are described as complementary tools in Lecture 2. What is the correct distinction between them?

options.text: PESTEL evaluates the firm's internal competencies; Five Forces evaluates the industry's external attractiveness
PESTEL scans the macro-environment (General Environment); Five Forces diagnoses competitive dynamics of the specific industry (Operating/Industry Environment)
PESTEL is a quantitative model; Five Forces is a qualitative framework that cannot be scored
PESTEL is applied after product launch; Five Forces is used only during the NPD concept generation phase

questions.explanation: PESTEL addresses the outer General Environment ring (macro-level: Social, Economic, Political, Technological, Environmental, Legal factors). Porter's Five Forces addresses the Operating/Industry Environment — the specific competitive dynamics of the industry (new entrants, buyers, suppliers, substitutes, rivalry). Both are applied during Phase 1 opportunity evaluation. PESTEL uses a 1–5 favorability rating scale and is not inherently quantitative versus qualitative relative to Five Forces.

questions.question: Explain the Red Bull case as an example of 'imitate but differentiate' opportunity generation. What was the original product, what was the key reframing move, and what principle does this illustrate about opportunity sourcing?
questions.correctAnswer: Krating Daeng was a Thai energy drink popular among Thai truck drivers. Dietrich Mateschitz encountered it during a business trip, reformulated it (carbonated, new flavor profile), and repositioned it as a premium lifestyle brand for Western markets rather than a functional trucker's drink. The key reframing was identifying the underlying need (energy and alertness) and serving it in a new cultural context with aspirational positioning. This illustrates the 'import geographically isolated innovations' technique of opportunity sourcing — a form of external pull innovation where the source is not customers or competitors in the home market but a product already validated in another geography.

questions.question: In the Concept Scoring Matrix for the reusable insulin syringe case, which concept won and why was the result noteworthy?

options.text: Concept A (Master Cylinder) won because it scored highest on every individual criterion
Concept DF (Lever Stop) won with a total score of 3.45 — noteworthy because it was a combination of two concepts that had been individually eliminated in the screening stage
Concept G+ (enhanced Dial Screw) won because it had the best Dose Metering Accuracy rating
Concept E (Swash Ring) won because it had the lowest manufacturing cost

questions.explanation: Concept DF (Lever Stop) won the scoring stage with a total weighted score of 3.45, ranking first. The noteworthy finding was that DF was a combination of concepts D and F, both of which had been individually eliminated in the earlier screening stage — demonstrating that features from eliminated concepts can still contribute to the final selected concept. This illustrates why the goal of concept evaluation is creation (combining and refining) rather than mere selection.

questions.question: In the salsa conjoint study, the OLS regression coefficient for 'Extra-Hot' spiciness was +10.667 (with rank 1 = most preferred). What does this coefficient mean in plain language?

options.text: Extra-Hot salsa is ranked approximately 11 positions better (more preferred) than Mild salsa, all else equal
Extra-Hot salsa is ranked approximately 11 positions worse (less preferred) than Mild salsa, all else equal
Extra-Hot salsa increases consumer preference ratings by 10.667 points on a 10-point scale
Extra-Hot salsa has a 10.667% higher market share than Mild salsa

questions.explanation: Because the dependent variable is a rank where rank 1 = most preferred (lower rank = better), a positive coefficient means the attribute level increases the rank number, making it less preferred. Extra-Hot has a coefficient of +10.667, meaning it is ranked nearly 11 positions higher (worse) than Mild salsa. Option (a) reverses the sign interpretation. Options (c) and (d) confuse the ranking outcome with rating scales or market share.

questions.question: Using the salsa conjoint results, calculate the relative importance of Thickness given: Thickness range = 6.16, Spiciness range = 10.67, Color range = 1.00.

options.text: Approximately 5.6%
Approximately 34.6%
Approximately 59.8%
Approximately 45.2%

questions.explanation: Relative Importance = (Range_j / ΣRange_k) × 100. Total range = 6.16 + 10.67 + 1.00 = 17.83. Thickness importance = 6.16 / 17.83 × 100 = 34.6%. Color importance = 1.00 / 17.83 × 100 = 5.6% (option a). Spiciness importance = 10.67 / 17.83 × 100 = 59.8% (option c). Option (d) is a distractor with no basis in the data.

questions.question: In the hotel staycation conjoint study, the coefficient for OceanView was +5.83 and the coefficient for HighPrice (+$50) was −1.68. What is the willingness to pay (WTP) for an Ocean view upgrade over a Garden view?

options.text: Approximately $42
Approximately $61
Approximately $174
Approximately $291

questions.explanation: WTP = (Attribute utility gain / |Price coefficient|) × Price increment = (5.83 / 1.68) × $50 = 3.47 × $50 = $173.51 ≈ $174. The price coefficient maps to a $50 increment between the two price levels tested. Option (a) is the WTP for a room size upgrade (+$42). Option (b) is the WTP for a City view upgrade (+$61). Option (d) would be the result of incorrectly dividing by a $1 price increment.

questions.question: A conjoint study of laptops tests Weight (2 levels), Battery life (2 levels), and Brand (2 levels). How many product profiles are included in a full factorial design?

options.text: 6 profiles
8 profiles
12 profiles
18 profiles

questions.explanation: Full factorial profile count = product of all level counts = 2 × 2 × 2 = 8 profiles. Option (a) would be the result of adding (2+2+2), a common mistake. Option (c) matches the hotel example (2×3×2). Option (d) matches the salsa example (3×3×2).

questions.question: According to Lecture 4, why can the Concept Scoring Matrix produce a 'best average product' that is not actually the best concept for development?

options.text: The matrix uses internally derived weights rather than customer survey data, making it subjective
A concept that scores moderately well on every criterion can beat a concept that excels on the most important criteria but is weak on others, even when the latter better serves customer priorities
The 1–5 rating scale is not granular enough to differentiate between concepts that perform similarly
Teams always select the concept with the highest single-criterion rating rather than the highest total score

questions.explanation: The 'best average product' problem occurs when a concept that scores 3 or 4 on every criterion (moderate across the board) achieves a higher total weighted score than a concept that scores 5 on the most heavily weighted criterion but 2 on others. The matrix can mask a concept's brilliance on critical dimensions by averaging it against its weaknesses. This is why sensitivity analysis and concept combination — asking 'which features from losing concepts would you steal?' — are essential steps after scoring.

questions.question: Using the hotel conjoint regression model (Y = 2.08 + 1.42·LargeRoom + 2.05·CityView + 5.83·OceanView − 1.68·HighPrice), calculate the predicted preference score for a 50 m² room with Ocean view priced at $350/night. Show your work.
questions.correctAnswer: Ŷ = 2.08 + 1.42(1) + 2.05(0) + 5.83(1) + (−1.68)(1) = 2.08 + 1.42 + 0 + 5.83 − 1.68 = 7.65 out of 10. The room is 50 m² so LargeRoom = 1; it has Ocean view so OceanView = 1 and CityView = 0; the price is $350 (HighPrice level) so HighPrice = 1.

questions.question: The Benetton postponement case study demonstrates that moving the dyeing step to after knitting reduced unsold inventory from 33% to 8%. What is the fundamental supply chain principle this illustrates?

options.text: Reducing the number of product variants offered lowers inventory complexity
By deferring the differentiating step, firms only need to forecast aggregate demand rather than color-level demand, reducing inventory risk
Vertical integration of production consistently outperforms outsourcing for fashion brands
Dyeing garments after knitting is technologically superior because colors are more vibrant

questions.explanation: Postponement's core benefit is that Benetton only needed to forecast total sweater volume (an easier problem), not the color breakdown. By holding undyed stock, they could observe actual demand and dye accordingly, dramatically reducing the mismatch between supply and demand at the variant level. The principle is that the differentiating step is moved as late as possible to leverage real demand signals. Options (a), (c), and (d) are either incorrect or irrelevant to the strategic principle.

questions.question: Define poka-yoke and provide two examples from Lecture 5 that illustrate the concept. Explain what distinguishes poka-yoke from simply providing a user warning label.
questions.correctAnswer: Poka-yoke is a Japanese engineering concept meaning 'mistake-proofing' — building physical or procedural safeguards into a product or process so that errors are prevented or immediately detected before they cause harm, rather than relying on user behavior change. Examples from the lecture include: (1) asymmetric battery terminals that physically prevent incorrect insertion — the battery can only be placed one way; (2) diesel fuel nozzles sized differently from petrol nozzles — the wrong nozzle physically cannot fit, preventing wrong-fuel incidents; (3) polarized electrical plugs that can only be inserted in one orientation. The distinction from warning labels is that poka-yoke eliminates the possibility of the error entirely through design constraints, whereas a warning label still relies on the user reading, understanding, and complying with the instruction — leaving the error still possible.

questions.question: Explain why conjoint analysis is described as 'less appropriate' for new-to-the-world products. What condition must be met for conjoint analysis to produce valid, actionable results?
questions.correctAnswer: Conjoint analysis is less appropriate for new-to-the-world products because respondents cannot meaningfully evaluate attribute combinations they have never experienced or conceptualized. The technique relies on respondents making realistic trade-offs between attribute levels — if the attributes themselves are unfamiliar, stated preferences are not reliable guides to actual purchase behavior. The core condition for conjoint validity is the additive utility assumption: the product must be decomposable into independent attributes, each of which contributes separately to overall utility, with no strong cross-attribute interaction effects. Additionally, attributes must be determinant (actually drive choice), discrete and unambiguous, and independently combinable.

questions.question: According to Bain & Company data cited in Lecture 8, what combined share of the luxury market are Millennials and Gen Z consumers projected to represent by 2025?

options.text: Approximately 44%
Approximately 63%
Over 70%
Approximately 50%

questions.explanation: The Bain data shows Millennials + Gen Z combined at 44% in 2019, 63% in 2021, and projected to exceed 70% by 2025, accounting for 130% of the luxury market's growth. Option (a) is the 2019 figure; option (b) is the 2021 figure; option (d) is not a stated figure in the notes.

questions.question: Gucci's repositioning under Alessandro Michele is described as 'disruptive repositioning.' On the perceptual map used in the lecture, which direction did this repositioning move the brand?

options.text: From Contemporary to Classic positioning, targeting older consumers (50s+)
From Classic/older-consumer positioning sharply upward to Contemporary, targeting 30s consumers
From mass-market to exclusive positioning, narrowing the target demographic to ultra-high-net-worth individuals
From a Western luxury identity to an Asia-first identity, prioritizing Chinese consumer preferences

questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly describes the perceptual map with Classic-to-Contemporary on the vertical axis and consumer age on the horizontal axis. Under Michele, Gucci moved sharply upward into the Contemporary quadrant while targeting 30s consumers — away from the classic, older positioning of prior eras and competitors like LV and Chanel. This was a pronounced, deliberate strategic shift, not incremental evolution.

questions.question: The lecture identifies geographic concentration risk for luxury brands. By 2025, Chinese consumers by nationality were projected to account for what percentage of global personal luxury goods purchases?
questions.explanation: Bain & Company data shows Chinese consumers at 33% of global luxury purchases in 2019, declining to 23% in 2021 due to COVID-19 travel restrictions, and projected to reach 43% by 2025. Option (a) is the 2021 figure; option (b) is the 2019 figure; option (d) is not cited in the notes.

questions.question: In Lecture 6, Netflix's A/B testing of thumbnail artwork was cited as an example of the power of randomized experiments. What was the approximate magnitude of improvement Netflix observed from optimized artwork?

options.text: 1–5% increase in viewing rates
20–30% increase in viewing rates
50–60% increase in viewing rates
100% increase (doubling) of viewing rates

questions.explanation: The lecture states Netflix has observed 20–30% increases in viewing rates from optimized thumbnail artwork through A/B testing. Because assignments were random, these differences can be causally attributed to the artwork rather than pre-existing user preferences. Options (a), (c), and (d) are all incorrect magnitudes not cited in the lecture notes.

questions.question: In Lecture 7, brand consideration is identified as a subdimension of Brand Judgments in the CBBE pyramid. According to the lecture, what makes consideration particularly important compared to other judgment types like quality or credibility?

options.text: Consideration is easier to measure than quality, making it the most actionable brand metric
Consideration is described as a 'crucial filter' — even high quality and credibility cannot produce brand resonance if consumers do not find the brand personally relevant enough to consider it
Consideration is the only judgment type that can be directly influenced through advertising
Consideration determines the price premium a brand can command over generic alternatives

questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly calls brand consideration a 'crucial filter for brand equity.' No matter how positively a brand is judged on quality or credibility, if consumers do not find it personally relevant — if it is not in their consideration set — it cannot achieve brand resonance (loyalty, attachment, community, engagement) at Level 4 of the pyramid. Consideration is the gateway from positive perceptions to actual brand relationship.

questions.question: The lecture raises brand dilution risk in the context of Gucci entering the beauty segment. What mechanism makes luxury brand extensions into lower-price-point categories potentially harmful to core brand equity?

options.text: Lower-price categories attract price-sensitive customers who then complain about quality, damaging online reviews
Accessibility erodes the scarcity cues that underpin the brand's signaling value — if too many people can access the brand, its ability to communicate status weakens
Beauty products require different distribution infrastructure that conflicts with the mono-brand store strategy
Entering beauty categories forces luxury brands to compete directly with FMCG companies that have much larger marketing budgets

questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly explains that accessibility erodes signaling value — scarcity is the mechanism that makes luxury goods effective status signals. If too many consumers can access a brand through low-priced beauty products, its exclusivity collapses and the core leather goods and ready-to-wear lose their premium justification. Option (a) is not cited in the lecture. Option (c) may be operationally true but is not the brand equity mechanism discussed. Option (d) is a competitive concern unrelated to the dilution mechanism.

questions.question: Describe what the 'New Luxury Paradigm' means, contrasting it with 'Old Luxury' across at least three dimensions. What demographic shift is primarily driving this transformation?
questions.correctAnswer: The New Luxury Paradigm replaces the Old Luxury model of status-symbol, transactional, passive consumption through exclusive physical retail with an experiential, participatory, digitally native approach. Across dimensions: (1) Symbol vs. Experience — Old Luxury treats the product as a pure status symbol; New Luxury engages consumers experientially and values self-expression. (2) Passive vs. Participatory consumption — Old Luxury consumers bought and wore; New Luxury consumers share, co-create, and engage. (3) Physical-only vs. Omnichannel/Digital — Old Luxury restricted to controlled physical environments; New Luxury leverages online channels (online sales grew from $37.3B in 2019 to $70.1B in 2021, projected 30% of market by 2025). The primary demographic driver is the Millennial and Gen Z cohort shift — projected to exceed 70% of the luxury market by 2025 — who prioritize experience, authenticity, values alignment, and digital-first discovery over pure status signaling.

questions.question: In the Crawford NPD Five-Phase Model, what evaluation question is applied at the Phase 1 to Phase 2 transition (after Opportunity Identification)?

options.text: Should we market it? (Market Testing)
Is the idea worth screening? (Initial Review)
Should we try to develop it? (Full Screen)
Have we developed it? (Progress Report)

questions.explanation: Phase 1 asks 'Where should we look?' (Direction). At the Phase 1→2 boundary, the evaluation question is 'Is the idea worth screening?' — an Initial Review. Phase 2→3 asks 'Should we try to develop it?' (Full Screen). Phase 4→5 asks 'Have we developed it?' (Progress Reports). Phase 5 concludes with 'Should we market it?' (Market Testing).

questions.question: Research cited in Lecture 1 compares top-performing NPD firms (top 25%) with the bottom 75%. Which metric most dramatically separates the two groups?

options.text: Top firms spend 3× more on R&D than bottom firms
Top firms require only 4.5 ideas per successful product vs. 11.4 for bottom firms, and achieve an 82.2% vs. 52.9% success rate
Top firms launch 10× more products per year than bottom firms
Top firms use exclusively digital development processes while bottom firms rely on waterfall methods

questions.explanation: The Markham and Lee (2013) data shows top-25% firms achieve 82.2% NPD success rates (vs. 52.9%) and require only 4.5 ideas per successful product (vs. 11.4). They also derive 47.9% of sales and 48.5% of profits from new products. The dramatic difference in ideas-per-success (4.5 vs. 11.4) reflects the compounding effect of better opportunity selection and evaluation. R&D spend ratios, product launch volumes, and process methodologies are not the measures cited.

questions.question: In the Concept Scoring Matrix, what score does the reference concept receive on every criterion by definition?

options.text: 0 on every criterion
1 on every criterion (worst possible)
3 on every criterion (midpoint of the 1–5 scale)
5 on every criterion (best possible)

questions.explanation: In the Concept Scoring Matrix, the reference concept is the baseline by definition and receives a neutral score of 3 (Same as Reference) on all criteria. This contrasts with the Concept Screening Matrix (Pugh Matrix), where the reference receives 0 (+/0/−). A concept scoring above 3.0 on a weighted basis outperforms the reference; below 3.0 underperforms. Setting it to 1 or 5 would incorrectly define the reference as best or worst.

questions.question: In Lecture 6, the endowment effect experiment used Lindor chocolates. In the original Kahneman, Knetsch, and Thaler (1990) study, what was the gap between the median seller's minimum acceptable price and the median buyer's maximum WTP for a $6 coffee mug?

options.text: $0.50 — sellers asked $4.00, buyers offered $3.50
$2.75 — sellers asked $5.25, buyers offered $2.50
$4.00 — sellers asked $6.00, buyers offered $2.00
$1.50 — sellers asked $5.50, buyers offered $4.00

questions.explanation: The Kahneman, Knetsch, and Thaler (1990) experiment found the median seller's minimum acceptable price was $5.25 and the median buyer's maximum WTP was $2.50 — a gap of $2.75 despite the mug having an identical objective retail value of $6 for both groups. This asymmetry, driven purely by ownership status, is the endowment effect.

questions.question: In the Critical Path Method (CPM), activities on the critical path are defined as having zero slack. If activity B has ES = 0, EF = 9, LF = 9, and LS = 0, what is its slack and is it a critical activity?

options.text: Slack = 9; not critical
Slack = 0; critical activity
Slack = −9; the activity is over-constrained
Slack cannot be determined without knowing the project duration

questions.explanation: Slack S = LS − ES = 0 − 0 = 0 (equivalently, LF − EF = 9 − 9 = 0). With S = 0, activity B is a critical activity — any delay in it directly delays the entire project. This matches activity B in the hospital relocation example (duration 9 weeks, on the critical path B-D-H-J-K of 69 weeks).

questions.question: Explain the role of 'brand consideration' in the CBBE pyramid framework (Lecture 7). Why is it described as a 'crucial filter for brand equity,' and what happens to a brand that scores high on quality but low on consideration?
questions.correctAnswer: Brand consideration is a sub-dimension of Brand Judgments (Level 3, left side of the CBBE pyramid). It measures how personally relevant customers find the brand — whether they would actually consider purchasing it. It is described as a 'crucial filter' because even a brand that consumers judge as high quality and highly credible cannot achieve brand resonance (Level 4 — the ultimate goal of loyalty, attachment, community, and engagement) if it is not considered personally relevant to those consumers. A brand that scores high on quality but low on consideration has failed to translate its performance and imagery signals into an actual purchase consideration set for target consumers. This typically means the brand's identity and positioning are not connecting with the specific needs, lifestyle, or values of the target segment — the meaning layer (Level 2) is not translating into the right response at Level 3.
